region_of_origin: Global (widely consumed in Asia and internationally)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for packaged sweetened green tea beverage; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve bottle of packaged sweetened green tea is 500 ml (approximately 500 g), commonly consumed as a single beverage portion.

nutrient_source.title: Green Tea (Packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged green tea (D09020044). All per-100g values are as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for commercially packaged sweetened green tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 47 is estimated from the closest analogous beverage: sweetened tea (GI ~47, low category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 29.7) / 100 = 13.96 ≈ 14.1 for a 500 g serving (29.7 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (5.67 g sugar/100 g) exceeding the 5 g/100 g beverage sugar gate; independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines regardless of low sodium content (8 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this sweetened packaged green tea and choose the unsweetened (zero-sugar) variant, as the AHA 2018 SSB guideline identifies sugar-sweetened beverages as an independent blood pressure risk factor irrespective of sodium level.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 5.67 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre; qualifies as an SSB triggering the ADA SSB hard gate; liquid sugar is rapidly absorbed, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this sweetened packaged green tea entirely and substitute the unsweetened variant, as the ADA classifies sugar-sweetened beverages as contraindicated for blood glucose management due to rapid liquid sugar absorption and absence of fibre buffering.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar content (5.67 g/100 g) in a ready-to-drink beverage contributes to excess free sugar intake; green tea catechins (EGCG) provide modest cardioprotective antioxidant benefit, but the sugar load offsets this advantage in the sweetened formulation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this sweetened packaged green tea due to its added sugar content, which contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial dysfunction risk; the unsweetened variant retains the cardioprotective catechin benefit without the sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Green tea catechins and EGCG provide anti-inflammatory properties; however, added sugar (5.67 g/100 g) contributes to systemic inflammatory load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory catechins in green tea but should prefer the unsweetened variant to avoid the pro-inflammatory effect of added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged sweetened green tea is a ready-to-drink (RTD) beverage commercially produced by steeping green tea leaves or using green tea extract, then blending with water, sugar, and food additives before bottling or canning. It is widely sold in Singapore and across Asia in 500 ml bottles and 250–330 ml cans. The flavour profile is mildly grassy and vegetal from the green tea base, balanced by sweetness from added sugar (approximately 5.67 g per 100 ml). The beverage is light, clear to pale yellow-green in colour, and consumed chilled. Per 100 ml it provides 24 kcal, 5.94 g carbohydrate (5.67 g sugar), negligible protein and fat, and 8 mg sodium. Protein and fat are naturally zero as this is a sugar-and-water-based tea beverage with no protein or fat-contributing ingredients. Dietary fibre is zero as the liquid formulation contains no intact plant cell wall material. Green tea provides polyphenols including epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) and other catechins with documented antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Clinically, this product qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under ADA and AHA definitions due to its sugar content exceeding 5 g per 100 ml, making it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension. The unsweetened variant is preferred for health-conscious consumers. Commonly consumed as a mealtime or snack beverage throughout Singapore, Malaysia, Japan, and China.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in bottled green tea?
Can diabetics drink packaged green tea?
Is RTD green tea good for blood pressure?
Calories in a bottle of green tea
Is sweetened green tea an SSB?
Is canned green tea suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (canonical primary source for Singapore food records, cr_id D09020044, confidence 0.95). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 for sweetened tea, clearly documented.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero: this is a water-and-sugar-based tea beverage containing no protein-contributing ingredients; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. fat_g is naturally zero by product formulation: no fat-contributing ingredients are present in this RTD green tea; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero: liquid beverage with no intact plant fibre; confirmed per HPB. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in HPB Foodo for this beverage entry. potassium_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; estimated ~20 mg/100 ml based on general green tea beverage data but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1. water_g set to -1: not explicitly reported in HPB Foodo for this entry. vitamin_c_mg and all other micronutrients set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this packaged green tea entry. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate from sweetened tea data in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for this specific commercial product was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. This food is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) per ADA and AHA definitions (sugar > 5 g/100 ml). The unsweetened variant (sugar_g = 0) is clinically preferred for diabetes and hypertension management. Ontology: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code assigned based on best available match for green tea ready-to-drink beverage; SNOMED CT omitted as no defensible code was identified.
